You are free: to copy, distribute, display, and perform the work, and to make derivative works under the following conditions: you must attribute the work in the manner specified by the licensor; you may not use this work for commercial purposes; if you alter, transform, or build upon this work, you may distribute the resulting work only under a license identical to this one. For any reuse or distribution, you must make clear to others the license terms of this work. Any of these conditions can be waived if you get permission from the licensor. Your fair use and other rights are in no way affected by the above.

div.head: LECTURE XXI.

head.hi: And Israel said unto Joseph, I had not though so see thy face; and, lo! God hath showed me also thy seed.

div.p:
THE internal evidence of the truth of the Old as well as the New Testament, is by much too considerable to be overlooked. By internal evidence, of course, I mean those marks of reality and genuineness, which are produced by the probability, fitness, pertinence, connexion both of the speeches and actions therein occurring, and which are accommodated to the personages, the times in which they lived, the habits of their life, the circumstances which controlled, the motives which influenced them, in such a manner as no human invention has ever equalled, and no alteration could improve. Whoever is desirous of discovering, and capable of appreciating these marks of reality, these internal proofs of truth stamped on the Scriptures, no doubt, by the will and pmport of God who g-iveth us largely every method of improving our
faith, will find them no where more thickly sown than in the five concluding chapters of the book of Genesis. The collected wisdom of this world has not furnished in any other writings such an impressive description of every human feeling, whether it be of envy, cruelty, grief, resignation, gratitude, pardon, or piety, all working out one great scheme of good.

When it was related to Jacob, that his favourite son, whom he had so long and deeply deplored, that Joseph was yet alive, the workings of his mind are represented with a brevity, force, and truth, shaming the most laboured efforts of other ancient historians. Let me place the very words before you: "Jacob's heart fainted, for he believed them not."—This disbelief was not a disbelief in the power of God, but proceeded from a proper sense of his own unworthiness. What had he done to receive such great reward? Why should the Lord turn his sorrow into joy like the rivers of the south? He was therefore like one who did dream; he had not dared to hope that he should ever see all his children's children and peace upon Israel. Of the same nature was the pious doubt which overwhelmed the faculties of the disciples (when their crucified Redeemer reappeared among them), while they believed not for joy. Anon, however, when his sons had repeatedrepeat-
ed to him their wonderful tidings, and confirmed their truth by showing the presents of which themselves were bearers, then the spirit of Jacob their father revived; and Israel said, " It is enough; Joseph my son is yet alive: I will go and see him before I die."

Prosperity of any sort is a great trial of the heart, and indeed a very accurate test in general, whether it be well with God. The gift does not always remind us of the Giver. When we are magnified by any sudden visitation of good, be it ours to magnify the Lord; and when our spirit revives from the conflict of doubt, and swells into joy, let it habitually rejoice in the Lord our Saviour. Adversity seldom fails to cast us on our knees; for, when human friends forsake or look cool, the heart in very bitterness, as well as destitution, seeketh for a heavenly friend. But as Solomon says to those of immature years, "Remember thy Creator in the days of thy youth;" so docs the law of the Gospel, which is greater than Solomon, say unto all, Remember thy Creator in the day of rejoicing, lest your triumph be made a snare unto you. Jacob did remember the Lord with his whole heart at this most critical moment. He did not venture to indulge even what appeared to be the natural fondness of a parent, without mixing with it a truly pious feeling.
